planting.note: Plant rooted suckers or hardwood cuttings in early spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; quaking aspen is most commonly propagated by root suckers from existing trees, as seed viability is extremely short (germinates within days but loses viability within a week if not planted). Plant in masses for the natural clonal colony effect.

directions: Plant root suckers or cuttings in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; quaking aspen is the most widely distributed native tree in North America, ranging from Alaska to Newfoundland and south through the Rocky Mountains to Mexico. Named for the distinctive trembling of the leaves on their flattened petioles in even the slightest breeze, the constant movement produces a shimmering, glittering effect that is uniquely beautiful. Aspen groves are clonal colonies — all trees in a grove are genetically identical, connected by a single underground root system. The famous 'Pando' clone in Utah is estimated to be 80,000+ years old and weighing over 6 million kilograms, potentially the oldest living organism on Earth. Brilliant golden-yellow fall color; white bark provides winter interest. Best in cool, moist climates of the northern states and mountain West; struggles in hot, humid southern areas.

planting.note: Plant rooted suckers or cuttings in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bigtooth aspen is the eastern counterpart to quaking aspen, more common on drier, sandier soils in the Northeast and Great Lakes region. It also spreads by root suckers to form clonal colonies.

directions: Plant root suckers or cuttings in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; bigtooth aspen is distinguished from quaking aspen by its larger, more coarsely toothed leaves with larger, irregular, rounded teeth (reflecting the name 'grandidentata' — large-toothed). More common on sandy, dry soils and mixed hardwood-aspen forests of the Northeast and Great Lakes region. Like quaking aspen, it spreads clonally by root suckers to form groves, and the flat petioles cause the leaves to tremble in the breeze. The bark is greenish-white to cream-colored, somewhat less white than quaking aspen. Fall color is a warm golden-yellow. The bigtooth aspen grove supports important habitat structure — the trees are fast-growing pioneer species that provide early succession habitat for nesting birds and beaver activity. Excellent for naturalistic, large-scale native woodland restoration in appropriate cool climates.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings in late winter to early spring in moist, riparian, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Fremont cottonwood is a riparian specialist requiring consistent access to groundwater. Plant near streams, rivers, and natural waterways, or in sites with high water tables.

directions: Plant hardwood cuttings in spring in moist to wet, deep, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun along stream banks, river edges, and areas with high water tables; Fremont cottonwood is the dominant riparian tree of the American Southwest, forming gallery forests along desert rivers from California to New Mexico. The massive, fast-growing trees reach 40–80 feet, with deeply furrowed, gray-brown bark and large, triangular, bright green leaves that flutter constantly in desert breezes. In the arid Southwest, riparian cottonwood forests are oases of biodiversity — the trees support more bird species than any other habitat type in the desert, providing nesting sites for endangered southwestern willow flycatchers, yellow-billed cuckoos, and numerous neotropical migrants. The fall foliage is brilliant gold, providing the most spectacular fall color in the otherwise largely evergreen Sonoran and Mojave Desert landscapes. Named for explorer John C. Frémont who documented it in western expeditions.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to wet, compacted or disturbed, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; toad rush is a cosmopolitan annual of seasonally moist, disturbed, and compacted ground — path edges, tire ruts, pond margins, and mudflats. It establishes and sets seed rapidly before conditions dry out.

directions: Scatter seed on moist, compacted, disturbed soil at the margins of ponds, seasonal wetlands, and wet disturbed areas; toad rush is a tiny, nearly grass-like annual rush that grows naturally in the muddy margins of ponds, seasonal pools, tire tracks, path edges, and any temporarily moist, disturbed ground across virtually all of North America and most of the world. The very small stature and wiry stems bear tiny flowers along most of their length. While not ornamental, toad rush is an important ecological pioneer plant that quickly colonizes bare, moist, disturbed soil before other species establish, providing early ground cover and preventing erosion. The tiny seeds are eaten by various shorebirds and wading birds that forage in its typical shallow-water and mudflat habitat. It self-sows automatically in any appropriate seasonal mud habitat without management.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; marsh muhly grows naturally in moist prairies, wet meadow margins, calcareous seeps, and open wet woodlands across the northern US and Canada. It is one of the more shade-tolerant of the muhly grasses.

directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; marsh muhly is a more cold-hardy, moisture-loving muhly grass of moist prairies, wet meadow edges, and open calcareous fens across the northern US and Canada, differing from the warm-season ornamental muhlys in its preference for consistently moist soil and its more compact, clumping nature without the dramatic fall plume display of M. capillaris. The dense, upright stems bear tight, clustered (glomerata = ball-clustered) seed heads from midsummer that are attractive in a subtle, native-prairie way. More shade-tolerant than most muhlys and useful in partial shade in moist prairie or savanna edge settings. Provides important food and nesting material for prairie sparrows and other grassland birds. Hardy to zone 3, making it one of the most cold-tolerant muhly grasses.
